planting.note: Plant transplants after all frost danger has passed; in zone 9 plant March–May. Senorita Rosalita is a sterile, non-seeding hybrid cleome — an important improvement over the straight species (Cleome hassleriana) that self-seeds aggressively and can become weedy in zone 9 gardens. Being sterile, Senorita Rosalita blooms continuously throughout the season without the energy diversion to seed set that causes standard cleome to look ragged by midsummer. The plant is also free of the sticky, resinous substance that makes standard cleome unpleasant to handle. As a vegetatively propagated cultivar it is sold as transplants, not seed. Hummingbirds and swallowtail butterflies are strongly attracted to the flowers.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Senorita Rosalita Cleome thrives in hot, sunny conditions with minimal water and declines in partial shade or consistently moist, fertile soil that promotes excessive vegetative growth; Senorita Rosalita is a sterile, non-self-seeding hybrid Cleome producing the airy, spider-like flower heads of the traditional Spider Flower (Cleome hassleriana) in a compact, non-seeding, non-sticky-stemmed form that eliminates the primary nuisances of the standard species. The flowers — clusters of four-petaled, long-stamened, rose-pink blooms arranged in a rounded, spidery head on tall stems — are produced continuously from late spring through frost because the plant channels all its energy into continuous flowering rather than seed production. Hummingbirds are strongly attracted to cleome flowers, and swallowtail butterflies (Giant, Eastern Tiger, and Spicebush Swallowtail) visit reliably for nectar. The tall, open, airy structure of Senorita Rosalita provides a light, vertical accent in the back of summer borders — the stem-and-flower silhouette visible through the wind and providing a transparency that most tall summer annuals do not offer. In zone 9's long, hot summer, this sterile hybrid maintains its flowering display through the entire season without the decline and seed mess that limits standard Cleome.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Cleome for Florida Annual Gardens
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Heat-Tolerant Hummingbird Annuals for Zone 9
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Tall Summer Annuals for Gulf South Borders

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant after all frost danger has passed; in zone 9 sow March–May. Gnome Pink is one of the most compact gomphrena selections available — reaching only 6–9 inches with a spreading, mounding habit, producing globe-shaped, clover-like, rose-pink flowers on short stems. The papery, dry-textured flower heads are naturally long-lasting both on the plant and as dried flowers — maintaining their color and shape for months when cut and dried. In zone 9 gomphrena is one of the best-adapted heat-and-humidity-tolerant summer annuals available, actively thriving in conditions that reduce or stop flowering in many other annuals. The plants are essentially unaffected by zone 9 summer heat, continuing to produce abundant flowers through July–September when the heat discourages many annuals. Excellent for dried flower arrangements.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Gnome Pink Gomphrena thrives in heat and poor-to-moderate soil conditions and declines in overly fertile, wet situations; Gnome Pink is a dwarf compact selection of Globe Amaranth (Gomphrena globosa), producing the distinctive clover-like, rounded, papery flower heads in bright rose-pink at a miniature scale — just 6–9 inches tall — that makes it practical for edging, rock gardens, small containers, and front-of-border situations where taller gomphrena selections would be out of scale. The flower heads of gomphrena are composed of papery, everlasting bracts surrounding tiny true flowers — the bracts providing the color and texture that are both attractive in the garden and naturally suited to drying, as the papery texture means the flowers hold their color and form after cutting and air-drying without the wilting and discoloration that affects more conventional floral textures. Globe Amaranth is one of the most genuinely heat-adapted summer annuals for zone 9 — a tropical species from Central America and South Asia that actively grows and flowers through the hottest conditions zone 9 can produce, while also being drought tolerant after establishment, requiring minimal care, and providing continuous flower production through the entire summer growing season.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Gomphrena globosa for Florida Annual Gardens
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Heat-Tolerant Summer Annuals for Zone 9
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Globe Amaranth for Gulf South Summer Color

planting.note: Plant transplants in spring as soon as last hard frost danger passes; in zone 9 plant February–April. Superbena is a trailing verbena series bred for superior heat tolerance, powdery mildew resistance, and continuous flowering without deadheading — improvements over older verbena selections that were problematic in Gulf South summers due to mildew and heat decline. As a vegetatively propagated cultivar it is sold as transplants, not seed. In zone 9 trailing verbena performs best February–May and September–November; midsummer heat may reduce flowering but the plants recover in fall. The burgundy-red color provides an intense, jewel-toned warm color for mixed container compositions. Excellent butterfly and pollinator plant.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil with consistent moisture — Superbena Burgundy Verbena requires full sun for maximum flowering and good air circulation to prevent powdery mildew; Superbena Burgundy is a trailing verbena hybrid producing clusters of small, five-petaled, rich burgundy-red to deep wine-red flowers in dense, rounded heads on trailing stems — creating a cascading display of intense, saturated warm color that is particularly effective in elevated containers, hanging baskets, and raised beds where the trailing stems can cascade freely. The Superbena series was developed specifically for improved performance over older trailing verbena in hot, humid conditions — addressing the primary failure mode of older verbena (powdery mildew and summer heat decline) through breeding rather than fungicide management. Verbena flowers are among the most effective pollinator plants available for warm-season annuals — the flat-topped, nectar-rich flower clusters attract a wide range of beneficial insects including bees, skippers, painted ladies, and fiery skippers throughout the bloom season. The rich, jewel-toned burgundy color of Superbena Burgundy provides a deep, saturated warm tone that pairs elegantly with yellow, white, and silver companions while providing strong color presence in mixed container compositions from late winter through the first flush of summer heat.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Trailing Verbena for Florida Containers
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Heat-Tolerant Verbena for Gulf South
Proven Winners — Superbena Series Verbena Performance Data

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is warm; in zone 9 sow March–May. The Sundial series is one of the most widely grown portulaca series in North America, selected for flowers that remain open longer than older portulaca — traditional portulaca closes in shade and on cloudy days, while Sundial remains open in lower light conditions. Do not cover seed — portulaca requires light for germination. Direct sow on the soil surface and press in lightly. Thin seedlings to 8–10 inches. In zone 9 portulaca is one of the most dependably heat and drought-tolerant annuals available — thriving in the harshest summer conditions while continuing to flower prolifically. Excellent for rock gardens, sandy banks, and other hot, dry situations where few other annuals can perform.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, lean, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Portulaca demands full sun and excellent drainage; it thrives in the dry, hot conditions that defeat most other annuals and declines rapidly in shade or wet soil; Sundial Orange Portulaca is a compact, semi-double to double portulaca producing vivid orange flowers in the Sundial series, one of the most improved portulaca strains available — with flowers that remain open in lower light conditions than older portulaca varieties that close completely on cloudy days and in shade. The succulent stems and leaves of Portulaca grandiflora (from its South American native range) store water and enable the plant to withstand drought conditions at which most annual flowers wilt and decline — making portulaca uniquely valuable for hot, dry, full-sun situations including sandy banks, rock gardens, paving cracks, and dry border edges where conventional annuals would require frequent irrigation to survive. The brilliant orange of Sundial Orange provides one of the most saturated and vivid warm-color tones available from a low-growing annual — particularly effective as an edging or mass planting against stone or gravel surfaces where the orange flowers glow against neutral backgrounds. In zone 9's peak summer heat portulaca improves in performance while most other annuals decline, making it the go-to choice for reliably colorful, extremely low-maintenance summer color in the most challenging hot, dry sunny situations.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Portulaca grandiflora for Florida Annual Gardens
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Drought-Tolerant Annuals for Zone 9 Full Sun
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Low-Water Summer Annuals for Gulf South

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 16–20 weeks before desired outdoor transplant date; in zone 9 start seeds July–August for fall transplanting (September–October) for winter bloom, or start October–November for February–March transplanting for spring bloom. Lisianthus seed is extremely fine and often comes pelleted for easier handling — do not bury, as light is required for germination. The long lead time from seed to bloom (150–200 days) makes lisianthus one of the longest-season annuals. The Echo series is one of the most reliable lisianthus series for garden performance — bred for heat tolerance and resistance to botrytis. In zone 9 lisianthus blooms November–April; it declines in summer heat and is grown as a cool-season crop.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral (pH 6.5–7.5) soil with consistent moisture — Lisianthus is unusual among cut flower crops in preferring near-neutral or slightly alkaline soil conditions rather than the acidic soil preferred by most floriculture crops; Echo Blue Lisianthus is a leading commercial lisianthus cultivar producing the double, ruffled, blue-to-blue-violet flowers that represent one of the most elegant and sophisticated floral presentations available from any annual cut flower crop — the multiple layers of tissue-thin, ruffled petals and the rich blue color combining to create a flower that resembles a combination of a rose and a poppy in blue. Lisianthus (Eustoma grandiflorum) is a native wildflower of the US prairies and southern states that has been developed into one of the most valuable commercial cut flower crops in the world — the cut stems lasting 1–3 weeks in a vase, making them among the longest-lasting of all cut flowers. The blue-to-purple color range of Echo Blue is particularly valuable in cut flower work where true blue flowers are rare and high-value. For zone 9 gardeners growing lisianthus as a cool-season annual (November–April), the long growing time required is rewarded with one of the most sophisticated and beautiful cut flower crops available from any home garden.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Eustoma grandiflorum for Florida Cut Flower Production
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Lisianthus for Gulf Coast Cut Flower Gardens
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Cool-Season Cut Flower Crops for Gulf South
